# ADFA/DFA Report - Reversible Markdown Format

This document represents the structure, data, formulas, and formatting of the original Excel file in a reversible Markdown format.

---

## Sheet: Revision History for Template

- **State**: `visible`
- **Merged Cells**: `A1:D2`

### Column Dimensions
```json
{
  "A": {
    "width": "8.80",
    "hidden": false
  },
  "B": {
    "width": "21.90",
    "hidden": false
  },
  "C": {
    "width": "59.30",
    "hidden": false
  },
  "D": {
    "width": "27.90",
    "hidden": false
  }
}
```

### Row Dimensions
```json
{
  "1": {
    "height": "18.65",
    "hidden": false
  },
  "4": {
    "height": "17.50",
    "hidden": false
  },
  "5": {
    "height": "29.50",
    "hidden": false
  }
}
```

### Cell Data, Formulas, and Styles
| Cell | Value (Displayed) | Formula | Style (JSON) |
|:-----|:------------------|:--------|:-------------|
| A1 | DFA報告<br>DFA Report |  | `{"font": {"name": "Calibri", "size": 14.0, "bold": true, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "center", "vertical": "center", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| A4 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "Calibri", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "center", "vertical": "center"}}` |

| B3 | ASIL decomposition |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial (W1)", "size": 9.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "center", "vertical": "center", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| C3 | apportioning of redundant safety (3.132) requirements to elements (3.41), with sufficient independence (3.78), conducing to the same safety goal (3.139), with the objective of reducing the ASIL (3.6) of the redundant safety (3.132) requirements that are allocated to the corresponding elements (3.41)<br>Note 1 to entry: ASIL decomposition is a basis for methods of ASIL (3.6) tailoring during the design process (defined as requirements decomposition with respect to ASIL (3.6) tailoring in ISO 26262-9).<br>Note 2 to entry: ASIL Decomposition does not apply to random hardware failure requirements per ISO 26262-9:2018.<br>Note 3 to entry: Reducing the ASIL (3.6) of the redundant safety (3.132) requirements has some exclusions, e.g. confirmation measures (3.23) remain at the level of the safety goal (3.139). |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| C5 | failure (3.50) of an element (3.41) of an item (3.84) resulting from a root cause (inside or outside of the element ( 3.41)) and then causing a failure of another element (3.41) or elements (3.41) of the same or different item (3.84)<br>Note to entry: Cascading failures are dependent failures (3.29) that could be one of the possible root causes of a common cause failure (3.18). |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| C6 | failure (3.50) of two or more elements (3.41) of an item (3.84) resulting directly from a single specific event or root cause which is either internal or external to all of these elements (3.41)<br>Note to entry: Common cause failures are dependent failures (3.29) that are not cascading failures (3.17). See Figure 3. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| B7 | Common Mode Failure<br>CMF |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "center", "vertical": "center", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| C7 | case of CCF (3.18) in which multiple elements (3.41) fail in the same manner<br>Note to entry: Failure (3.50) in the same manner does not necessarily mean that they need to fail exactly the same. How close the failure modes (3.51) need to be in order to be classified as common mode failure depends on the context<br>EXAMPLE 1 A system (3.163) has two temperature sensors which are compared with each other. If the difference between the two temperature sensors is larger than or equal to 5°C it is handled as a fault (3.54) and the system (3.163) is switched into a safe state (3.131). A common mode failure lets both temperature sensors fail in such a way that the difference between the two sensors is smaller than 5°C and therefore is not detected.<br>EXAMPLE 2 In a CPU lockstep architecture (3.1) where the outputs of both CPUs is compared cycle by cycle, both CPUs need to fail exactly the same way in order for the failure (3.50) to go undetected. In this context, a common mode failure lets both CPU fail exactly the same way.<br>EXAMPLE 3 An over voltage failure (3.50) due to lots of parts not meeting their specification for over voltage is a common mode failure. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| C10 | failures (3.50) that are not statistically independent, i.e. the probability of the combined occurrence of the failures (3.50) is not equal to the product of the probabilities of occurrence of all considered independent failures (3.50)<br>Note 1 to entry: Dependent failures can manifest themselves simultaneously, or within a sufficiently short time interval, to have the effect of simultaneous failures (3.50).<br>Note 2 to entry: Dependent failures include common cause failures (3.18) and cascading failures (3.17).<br>Note 3 to entry: Whether a given failure (3.50) is a cascading failure (3.17) or a common cause failure (3.18) may depend on the hierarchical structure of the elements (3.41).<br>Note 4 to entry: Whether a given failure (3.50) is a cascading failure (3.17) or a common cause failure (3.18) may depend on the temporal behaviour of the elements (3.41).<br>Note 5 to entry: Dependent failures can include software failures (3.50) even if the probability of the failure (3.50) is not calculated. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| B12 | diagnostic coverage |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial (W1)", "size": 9.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "center", "vertical": "center", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| C12 | percentage of the failure rate (3.53) of a hardware element (3.41), or percentage of the failure rate (3.53) of a failure mode (3.51) of a hardware element (3.41) that is detected or controlled by the implemented safety mechanism (3.142)<br>Note 1 to entry: Diagnostic coverage can be assessed with regard to residual faults (3.125) or with regard to latent multiple-point faults (3.97) that might occur in a hardware element (3.41).<br>Note 2 to entry: Safety mechanisms (3.142) implemented at different levels in the architecture (3.1) can be considered.<br>Note 3 to entry: Except when it is explicitly mentioned, the proportion of safe faults (3.130) of a safety-related hardware element (3.41) is not considered when determining the diagnostic coverage of the safety mechanism (3.142). |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| C13 | different solutions satisfying the same requirement, with the goal of achieving independence (3.78)<br>Note 1 to entry: Diversity does not guarantee independence (3.78), but can deal with certain types of common cause failures (3.18).<br>Note 2 to entry: Diversity can be a technical solution (diverse hardware components (3.21), diverse SW components (3.21)) or a technical means (e.g. diverse compiler) to apply.<br>Note 3 to entry: Diversity is one of a way to realize of redundancy (3.122).<br>EXAMPLE Diverse programming; diverse hardware. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| C14 | failure (3.50) resulting from the combination of two independent hardware faults (3.54) that leads directly to the violation of a safety goal (3.139)<br>Note 1 to entry: Dual-point failures are multiple-point failures (3.96) of order 2.<br>Note 2 to entry: Dual-point failures that are addressed in the ISO 26262 series of standards include those where one fault (3.54) affects a safety-related element (3.144) and another fault (3.54) affects the corresponding safety mechanism (3.142) intended to achieve or maintain a safe state (3.131). |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| C23 | abnormal condition that can cause an element (3.41) or an item (3.84) to fail<br>Note 1 to entry: Permanent, intermittent, and transient faults (3.173) (especially soft errors) are considered.<br>Note 2 to entry: When a subsystem is in an error (3.46) state it could result in a fault for the system (3.163).<br>Note 3 to entry: An intermittent fault occurs from time to time and then disappears again. This type of fault can occur when a component (3.21) is on the verge of breaking down or, for example, due to an internal malfunction in a switch. Some systematic faults (3.165) (e.g. timing irregularities) could lead to intermittent faults. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| B24 | fault injection |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "center", "vertical": "center", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| C24 | method to evaluate the effect of a fault (3.54) within an element (3.41) by inserting faults (3.54), errors (3.46), or failures (3.50) in order to observe the reaction by observation points (3.101)<br>Note to entry: Fault injection can be performed at various levels of abstraction including item (3.84) or element (3.41) level depending on the scope, feasibility, observability and level of required detail. Depending on purpose, it can be performed at different stages of the safety lifecycle and by considering different fault models (3.58).<br>EXAMPLE 1 Injecting faults (3.54) during operation to verify that a safety mechanism (3.142) is working properly as part of a strategy to detect latent faults (3.85).<br>EXAMPLE 2 Injecting faults (3.54) during integration test through hardware debug ports or through dedicated software commands to test the hardware-software interface (HSI).<br>EXAMPLE 3 Simulating stuck-at faults (3.54) or transient faults at hardware component level to verify the diagnostic coverage (3.33) of a safety mechanism (3.142) or to identify faults (3.54) which may result in errors (3.46) or failures (3.50). |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| B26 | Fault Tolerant Time Interval<br>FTTI |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "center", "vertical": "center", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| C26 | Minimum time span from the occurrence of a fault (3.54) in an item (3.84) to a possible occurrence of a<br>hazardous event (3.77), if the safety mechanisms (3.142) are not activated<br>See Figure 5.<br>Note 1 to entry: The minimum time span is to be evaluated over all hazardous events (3.77). It can depend on<br>the characterization of the hazards (3.75).<br>Note 2 to entry: FTTI is related to a hazard (3.75) caused by a malfunctioning behaviour (1.86) of the item<br>(3.84). FTTI is a relevant attribute for safety goals (3.139) derived from this hazard (3.75).<br>Note 3 to entry: A fault (3.54) is timely covered by a safety mechanism (3.142), if the item (3.84) is maintained<br>in a safe state (3.131), or if the item (3.84) is transitioned to a safe state (3.131), or is transitioned to an emergency operation (3.43), within the relevant fault tolerant time interval.<br>Note 4 to entry: The occurrence of a hazardous event (3.77) is dependent on a fault (3.54) being present and a vehicle being in a scenario that allows the fault (3.54) to affect vehicle behaviour.<br>EXAMPLE A failure (3.50) in the brake system (3.163) may not result in a hazardous event (3.77) until the brakes are applied.<br>Note 5 to entry: While the FTTI is defined only at the item (3.84) level, at the element (3.41) level the maximum fault handling time interval (3.56) and the state to be achieved after fault handling to support the functional safety concept (3.68) can be specified.<br>Note 6 to entry: The fault detection time interval (3.55) may include multiple diagnostic test time intervals (3.35) to allow de-bouncing of errors (3.46) if the diagnostic test time interval (3.35) is sufficiently shorter than the fault detection time interval (3.55). |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| C41 | failure (3.50) that can occur unpredictably during the lifetime of a hardware element (3.41) and that follows a probability distribution<br>Note 1 to entry: Random hardware failure rates can be predicted with reasonable accuracy.<br>Note 2 to entry: Physical hardware failures (3.50) as defined by the PoF (3.111) methodology (SAE J1211, JEDEC JEP122, or similar) can be considered as random hardware failures for the purpose of this standard. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| C43 | existence of means in addition to the means that would be sufficient to perform a required function or to represent information<br>Note 1 to entry: Redundancy is used in the ISO 26262 series of standards with respect to achieving a safety goal (3.139) or a specified safety (3.132) requirement, or to representing safety-related information.<br>Note 2 to entry: The redundancy could be implemented homogenously or with diversity (3.37).<br>EXAMPLE 1 Duplicated functional components (3.21) can be an instance of redundancy for the purpose of increasing availability (3.7) or allowing fault (3.54) detection.<br>EXAMPLE 2 The addition of parity bits to data representing safety-related information provides redundancy for the purpose of allowing fault (3.54) detection. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| C44 | portion of a random hardware fault (3.119) that by itself leads to the violation of a safety goal (3.139), occurring in a hardware element (3.41), where that portion of the random hardware fault (3.119) is not controlled by a safety mechanism (3.142)<br>Note to entry: This presumes that the hardware element (3.41) has safety mechanism (3.142) coverage for only a portion of its faults (3.54).<br>EXAMPLE If a set of faults (3.54) which is safety-relevant and not safe has a subset with 60% coverage, then the remaining 40% of the set of faults (3.54) are residual faults. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| C45 | fault (3.54) whose occurrence will not significantly increase the probability of violation of a safety goal (3.139)<br>Note 1 to entry: As shown in ISO 26262-5:2018, Annex B, both non-safety and safety-related elements (3.144) can have safe faults.<br>Note 2 to entry: Single-point faults (3.156), residual faults (3.125) and dual-point faults (3.39) do not constitute safe faults.<br>Note 3 to entry: Unless shown relevant in the safety (3.132) concept, multiple-point faults (3.97) with higher order than 2 can be considered as safe faults (3.130). |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| C48 | technical solution implemented by E/E functions or elements (3.41), or by other technologies (3.105), to detect and mitigate or tolerate faults (3.54) or control or avoid failures (3.50) in order to maintain intended functionality (3.83) or achieve or maintain a safe state (3.131)<br>Note 1 to entry: Safety mechanisms are implemented within the item (3.84) to prevent faults (3.54) from leading to single-point failures (3.155) and to prevent faults (3.54) from being latent faults (3.85).<br>Note 2 to entry: The safety mechanism is either<br>a) able to transition to, or maintain, the item (3.84) in a safe state (3.131), or<br>b) able to alert the driver such that the driver is expected to control the effect of the failure (3.50), as defined in the functional safety concept (3.68). |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| A2 | Dependent failure is an event, of which occurrence probability cannot be modelled as a product of single occurrence probabilities (mathematical), or event, which is caused by any interdependent structure (multiple failure, technical). Dependent failures can be classified in the following categories:<br><br>• CCF   (Common Cause Failure). Type of a dependent failure, at which a common single cause triggers several failures occurring simultaneously.<br>• CMF  (Common Mode Failure). Specific CCF, in which several units or components fail in the same way.<br>• CF     (Cascading Failures). Failures causing another element to fail.<br>Note: Dependent failures concern not only redundant (parallel) systems but all systems where a combination of events (cut sets 2nd and higher order) may violate a safety goal.<br><br>There are two basic approaches to analyse the dependent failures:<br><br>• Event specific models                                    (initial point: root causes for dependent failures  ->  impact analysis on redundant elements)<br>  -Consideration of special consequences resulting from predefined events as for example environmental impact, common supply, human factor, <br>   technology used and analysing their impact on the redundant system elements<br><br>• Event tree and fault tree cut set analysis   (initial point: cut sets estimated -> analysis of dependant failure sources which could have impact on these cut sets)<br>  Consideration of logical AND combinations (cut sets)  between the elements and analysing causes which could impact these combinations<br>   <br>Both approaches complement one another: <br>- the event specific model  investigates pre-defined event's impact on redundant elements of the system<br>- the cut set analysis discloses the concatenation of the cut set elements and investigates events which could have impact on these cut sets<br> |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| B2 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"top": "thin"}}` |

| A6 | ISO 26262-10 Annex A.3.6 gives an example on possible proceeding when performing DFA.<br>The Dependent Failure Analysis is structured into the following steps:<br><br>1) Identification of events (parts) could be subject to dependent failure can be done:<br>    - based on analysis of the Functional Safety Concept, system architecture where structures or parts claimed to be independent of each other in fact can<br>      be susceptible to dependent failures<br>    - based on deductive analysis such as FTA which allows cut set analysis - i.e. estimation of events which are combined by logical AND combination and<br>      which when all events occur violate the safety goal.<br>     These cut sets represent redundant logical structures. Some examples of such cut sets 2nd order (& symbol represents logical AND  between them):<br>    - µcontroller's failure & watchdog's failure<br>    - main µcontroller's failure & co-controller's failure<br>    - sensor A failure & sensor B failure<br><br>2) Identification of sources for potential dependent failures.<br><br>The table below implements and expands ISO 26262-10 Table A.7 in view of topics for dependant failure evaluation, potential initiators and related measures |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| B6 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}}` |
| C6 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}}` |
| D6 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}}` |

| A2 | Proceeding:<br>1) Estimation of Cut Sets based on Fault Tree Analysis performed for each safety goal (the Cut Sets are output by the FTA tool as e.g. Isograph Fault Tree+). Remark: FTA is an input for a DFA and not output!<br>    The Cut Sets output by FTA tool often result in numerous amount of Cut Sets even for a clear FTA structure and limited Cut Set order.<br>    Remark: the Cut Set analysis can be limited to a pre-defined Cut Set order e.g. 3 - the higher the Cut set order the less is the probability, that all Cut Set events are impact by the same CCF. However, the Cut Set order limitation has to be performed carefully.<br>    An analysis of every Cut set can be time consuming.  On that reason a CCCI – Common Cause Criticality Index is introduced and the events contained in each cut set are analysed in view of following issues:<br><br>    a) Spatial resolution. Distance between the elements involved in a cut set events. Example: IC pins close to each other can be impact by the same factor (metal particles, dust in combination with humidity, salt mist …) and effect a common stuck-at failure<br>    b) Sharing of common resources for elements in a cut set. Example: power supply generated by the same voltage regulator or ASIC for different redundant processing elements, same time base (clock) provided for a microprocessor and for a watchdog <br>        controlling its program sequence<br>    c) Conducting of the same information (same signal passes cut set elements). Example: MOSFETS in a H-bridge leading the same motor current, relay contacts in the same power circuit<br>    d) Same function and the same technology (common mode for the cut set elements). Example: MOSFETs with similar environmental and functional characteristics in view of their max. allowable junction temperature, microprocessors in the same <br>          technology being subjected by the same conducted and radiated signals, relays contacts with the same affection on welding, sensors based on same physical functioning<br>    e) Components located in the same package (housing of the cut set elements). Example: logic elements, operational amplifier, bipolar transistors located in the same package, on-chip watchdog, relays<br> |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| A10 | For each issue (a till e) a score between 0 (not applicable at all) and 3 (highly applicable) is assigned.  The addition of scores results in Common Cause Criticality Index (CCCI) value. The higher the CCCI value, the higher the common cause impact between the cut set elements could be.<br>High CCCI value is an indication for several issues contributing to a common cause potential which needs further investigation (e.g. in a FMEA, ) or additional measures (e.g. environmental testing, robustness test, re-design...)<br>The CCCI-value itself does not represent an exact quantitative limit for a common cause potential – it is rather a rough highlighting / focusing on Cut Sets where several issues match for further, more detailed investigation.<br>For high CCCI values additional "Detection / prevention measures. Further investigations" have to be specified.<br> |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

| A9 | Alternate techniques/measures are indicated by a letter following the number. Only one of the alternate techniques/measures can be selected.<br>Techniques and measures listed in this table are not exhaustive. Other techniques and measures may be used, provided evidence is given to support the claimed delta β-factor.<br>If evidence can be provided that measures were taken to mitigate the impact of common cause failures, other delta β-factors may be used. General advice from Annex D of IEC 61508-6 should be observed in such cases.<br>NOTE The interface signals between the redundant blocks are generally composed of multiple layers. Irrespective of the composition of a signal, whether it is solely constructed with only one metal layer or it is a mix of multiple layers, the whole interface signal will be considered as a single wire. To minimise possible interference of both channels by one fault none of the interface signals should cross over with the rest of the interface signals. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 9.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |

### Cell Data, Formulas, and Styles
| Cell | Value (Displayed) | Formula | Style (JSON) |
|:-----|:------------------|:--------|:-------------|
| A1 | 7 Analysis of dependent failures |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "bold": true, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"vertical": "center"}}` |
| A2 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 12.0}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A3 | 7.1 Objectives |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "bold": true, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"vertical": "center"}}` |
| A4 | The analysis of dependent failures aims to identify the single events or single causes that could bypass or invalidate a required independence or freedom from interference between given elements and violate a safety requirement or a safety goal. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| A5 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A6 | 7.2 General |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "bold": true, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"vertical": "center"}}` |
| A7 | The analysis of dependent failures considers architectural features such as:<br>⎯ similar and dissimilar redundant elements;<br>⎯ different functions implemented with identical software or hardware elements;<br>⎯ functions and their respective safety mechanisms;<br>⎯ partitions of functions or software elements;<br>⎯ physical distance between hardware elements, with or without barrier;<br>⎯ common external resources.<br>According to the definitions given in ISO 26262-1, independence is threatened by common cause failures and cascading failures, while freedom from interference is only threatened by cascading failures.<br>EXAMPLE 1 A high intensity electromagnetic field that causes different electronic devices to fail in a way that depends on design and use is an example of a common cause failure. Biased vehicle speed information that affects the behaviour of one or more vehicle functions is an example of cascading failures.<br>Dependent failures can manifest themselves simultaneously, or within a sufficiently short time interval, to have the effect of simultaneous failures.<br>EXAMPLE 2 A monitor designed to detect anomalous behaviour of a function can be rendered inoperative some time before the monitored function fails if both the monitor and the monitored function are subjected to the same event or cause. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| A8 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A9 | 7.3 Inputs to this clause |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "bold": true, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"vertical": "center"}}` |
| A10 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 11.0}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A11 | 7.3.1 Prerequisites |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A12 | The following information shall be available:<br>⎯ the independence requirements at the level at which they are applied: system, or hardware, or software in accordance with ISO 26262-3:2011, 8.5.1, or ISO 26262-4:2011, 6.5.1, or ISO 26262-5:2011, 6.5.1, or ISO 26262-6:2011, 6.5.1;<br>⎯ the freedom from interference requirements at the level at which they are applied: system, or hardware, or software in accordance with ISO 26262-3:2011, 8.5.1, or ISO 26262-4:2011, 6.5.1, or ISO 26262-5:2011, 6.5.1, or ISO 26262-6:2011, 6.5.1; <br>⎯ and the architectural information at the level at which the independence or freedom from interference requirements are to be applied: system, or hardware, or software in accordance with ISO 26262-4:2011, 7.5.2, or ISO 26262-5:2011, 7.5.1, or ISO 26262-6:2011, 7.5.1.<br>NOTE The architectural information is used to determine the boundaries of the analyses of dependent failures. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| A13 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A14 | 7.3.2 Further supporting information |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A15 | None. |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A16 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A17 | 7.4 Requirements and recommendations |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "bold": true, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"vertical": "center"}}` |
| A18 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 11.0}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A19 | 7.4.1 The potential for dependent failures shall be identified from the results of safety analyses in accordance with Clause 8.<br>NOTE 1 Both systematic failures and random hardware failures have the potential to be dependent failures.<br>NOTE 2 The identification of potential for dependent failures can be based on deductive analyses: examination of cut sets or repeated identical events of an FTA can indicate potential for dependent failures.<br>NOTE 3 The identification can also be supported by inductive analyses: similar parts or components with similar failure modes that appear several times in an FMEA can give additional information about the potential for dependent failures. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| A20 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A21 | 7.4.2 Each identified potential for dependent failures shall be evaluated to determine its plausibility, i.e. if a reasonably foreseeable cause exists which leads to the dependent failure and consequently violates a required independence or freedom from interference between given elements.<br>NOTE When quantification of random hardware failures is required, as for the evaluation of the safety goal violations due to random hardware failures (see ISO 26262-5), the contribution of common cause failures is estimated on a qualitative basis because no general and sufficiently reliable method exists for quantifying such failures. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| A22 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A23 | 7.4.3 This evaluation shall consider the operational situations as well as the different operating modes of the item or element being analyzed. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| A24 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A25 | 7.4.4 This evaluation shall consider the following topics as applicable:<br>NOTE 1 The evaluation of the potential dependent failures plausibility can be supported by appropriate checklists, e.g. checklists based on field experience. The checklists provide the analysts with representative examples of root causes and coupling factors such as: same design, same process, same component, same interface, proximity. IEC 61508 provides information that can be used as a basis to establish such checklists.<br>NOTE 2 This evaluation can also be supported by the adherence to process guidelines which are intended to prevent the introduction of root causes and coupling factors that could lead to dependent failures.<br>a) random hardware failures;<br>EXAMPLE Failures of common blocks such as clock, test logic and internal voltage regulators in large scale integrated circuits (microcontrollers, ASICs, etc.).<br>b) development faults;<br>EXAMPLE Requirement faults, design faults, implementation faults, faults resulting from the use of new technologies and faults introduced when making modifications.<br>c) manufacturing faults;<br>EXAMPLE Faults related to processes, procedures and training; faults in control plans and in monitoring special characteristics; faults related to software flashing and end-of-line programming.<br>d) installation faults;<br>EXAMPLE Faults related to wiring harness routing; faults related to the inter-changeability of parts; failures of adjacent items or elements.<br>e) repair faults;<br>EXAMPLE Faults related to processes, procedures and training; faults related to trouble shooting; faults related to the inter-changeability of parts and faults due to backward incompatibility.<br>f) environmental factors;<br>EXAMPLE Temperature, vibration, pressure, humidity / condensation, pollution, corrosion, contamination, EMC.<br>g) failures of common external resources; and<br>EXAMPLE Power supply, input data, inter-system data bus and communication.<br>h) stress due to specific situations.<br>EXAMPLE Wear, ageing. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| A26 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A27 | 7.4.5 Rationale for the plausibility of dependent failures and their impact shall be made available.<br>NOTE Plausible dependent failures are those for which the evaluation as given in 7.4.2 has revealed a reasonably foreseeable cause. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| A28 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A29 | 7.4.6 Measures for the resolution of plausible dependent failures shall be specified during the development phase, in accordance with the change management in ISO 26262-8. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| A30 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A31 | 7.4.7 Measures for the resolution of plausible dependent failures shall include the measures for preventing their root causes, or for controlling their effects, or for reducing the coupling factors.<br>EXAMPLE Diversity is a measure that can be used to prevent, reduce or detect common cause failures. |  | `{"font": {"name": "Arial", "size": 10.0}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top", "wrap_text": true}}` |
| A32 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
| A33 | 7.5 Work products |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "bold": true, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "fill": {"pattern": "solid", "fgColor": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"vertical": "center"}}` |
| A34 |  |  | `{"font": {"name": "\u65b0\u7d30\u660e\u9ad4", "size": 11.0, "color": "Values must be of type <class 'str'>"}, "border": {"left": "thin", "right": "thin", "top": "thin", "bottom": "thin"}, "alignment": {"horizontal": "left", "vertical": "top"}}` |
